title: Simple Pine Bookcase
summary: Five shelves, dadoed sides, a plywood back that does the real structural work. Cheap, fast, honest.
description:
The bookcase everyone should build second. It teaches dados and it teaches the single most under-appreciated fact in casework: the back panel is not trim, it's the thing that stops the whole case from folding over sideways.

Skip the back or tack it on loosely and you get a wobbly parallelogram. Glue and nail a plywood back into a rabbet and you get a case that will outlive the books.

Pine keeps it under $100. The same plan in oak or maple costs three times as much and works identically.

tools.note: Dados with a stacked dado set, or multiple passes with a regular blade.
An alternative to the table saw for the dados and the back rabbet.
For breaking down the plywood back sheet.

materials.name: Pine boards, 1x10 (3/4" x 9-1/4" actual)
materials.note: Sort through the pile at the store. Reject anything cupped, twisted, or with loose knots.

materials.name: Plywood, 1/4", for the back
materials.note: Half a sheet. This panel is what squares and stiffens the whole case — do not substitute cardboard-thin hardboard.

cutList.note: Length = 30" case width minus two 3/4" sides, plus 2 x 1/4" dado depth.

steps.title: Cut the sides and lay out the dados
steps.body:
Cut both sides to 60". Clamp them together, edges flush, and mark the dado positions **across both at once**. Marking them separately is how you end up with shelves that aren't level.

Space the shelves: bigger gaps at the bottom (13") for large books, tighter at the top (10").

steps.title: Cut the dados
steps.body:
3/4" wide, 1/4" deep, across the inside face of each side. Dado stack on the table saw, or a router with a straight bit and a clamped guide.

Test the fit on a scrap of your actual shelf stock — 'nominal 3/4" pine' is rarely exactly 3/4". Sneak up on a snug fit that you can push together by hand.

steps.title: Rabbet for the back
steps.body:
Rout a 1/4" x 1/4" rabbet down the back inside edge of both sides, and across the top piece. The plywood back sits in here, flush and hidden.

A back that's just nailed onto the rear edge looks like a shortcut, because it is one.

steps.title: Dry fit, then glue up
steps.body:
Assemble dry. Check the diagonals — if they're equal, the case is square.

Glue the shelves into the dados, clamp across the sides. Keep checking the diagonals as you tighten; clamps will pull a case out of square in seconds.

steps.title: Install the back
steps.body:
Cut the plywood to fit the rabbet. Glue and brad-nail it in, working around the perimeter and into every shelf's back edge.

**Square the case against the back panel:** push the case until the diagonals match, then nail the ply — the plywood locks that squareness in permanently. This is the moment the bookcase becomes rigid.

steps.title: Toe kick, sand, finish
steps.body:
Screw the toe kick between the sides at the bottom, set back 1". It keeps the case off dust and gives your feet somewhere to go.

Sand 120 → 220. Knock down the sharp edges. Pine drinks stain unevenly and blotchily — use a conditioner first, or just paint it, or leave it raw with a clear poly. Two coats.
